text: Female runners with a history of tibial stress fracture exhibit significantly greater instantaneous and average vertical loading rates during running compared to those without fracture history.
Female runners with a history of tibial stress fracture exhibit significantly greater peak tibial shock during running compared to those without fracture history.
The magnitude of tibial shock can predict tibial stress fracture history with 70% accuracy, suggesting it is a useful screening variable for fracture risk.
Tibial stress fractures typically require 6-8 weeks of recovery, making them among the most serious running injuries in terms of time lost.

Dynamic loading-related variables (vertical loading rates and tibial shock) rather than static structural measures are the key biomechanical factors associated with tibial stress fracture history in runners.
